label: The Problem of Suffering
text: The Problem of Suffering (Evil) as a challenge to theism

children.label: P1 — A perfect God would prevent all gratuitous suffering
children.text: P1 — If God is omnipotent, omniscient, and omnibenevolent, he would have both the power and the will to prevent any evil that serves no greater good. Such a God would not permit pointless pain.

children.label: The logical version: any evil is incompatible with God
children.text: J.L. Mackie argued the existence of any evil is logically incompatible with an omnipotent, wholly good God — at least one of these must be false.

children.label: The free will defense shows God and evil are compatible
children.text: Alvin Plantinga showed that God and evil are logically compatible: God, having good reason to create free beings, may be unable to create free beings who always freely choose good. The logical problem of evil is now widely regarded as defeated.

children.label: Free will doesn’t explain natural evil (earthquakes, cancer)
children.text: The free will defense covers moral evil (human choices), but natural evil — tsunamis, childhood cancer, animal suffering over millions of years before humans — cannot be attributed to human free choices.

children.label: Natural evil may involve spiritual agents or soul-making
children.text: Possible explanations: (1) malevolent spiritual agents whose free choices produce natural disorder; (2) Hick’s soul-making theodicy — a morally challenging environment is necessary for developing virtues like courage and compassion.

children.label: P2 — Gratuitous suffering appears to exist (the evidential problem)
children.text: P2 — Even granting logical compatibility, the sheer amount and distribution of suffering — horrific, seemingly pointless animal pain over millions of years before humans — makes God’s existence improbable. (Rowe’s evidential argument)

children.label: We cannot know any evil is genuinely gratuitous
children.text: Our cognitive limitations mean we cannot survey all possible goods and whether a given suffering serves them. The appearance of gratuitousness does not establish actual gratuitousness. (Skeptical theism — Wykstra, van Inwagen)

children.label: Skeptical theism leads to moral paralysis
children.text: If we can’t know whether suffering serves hidden divine goods, we also lose grounds to intervene to prevent it — perhaps our efforts foil God’s purposes.

children.label: Epistemic limits apply to God’s reasons, not our obligations
children.text: We act on our best available moral knowledge. Uncertainty about God’s providential purposes doesn’t undermine our obligation to relieve suffering — the two epistemic positions are different in kind.

children.label: ∴ Suffering is evidence against God, answered by theodicy
children.text: ∴ While the logical problem of evil is defeated, the evidential problem remains: the quantity and distribution of suffering constitute evidence against (though not disproof of) a perfectly good, omnipotent God. Theists must offer a credible theodicy.

children.label: Soul-making theodicy: suffering enables virtue and depth of character
children.text: John Hick’s soul-making theodicy: a world designed for comfort would produce shallow, dependent beings. Suffering, struggle, and adversity are necessary conditions for developing courage, compassion, perseverance, and deep love.

children.label: Eschatological hope: eternal life redeems finite suffering
children.text: From a Christian perspective, present suffering is finite while the joy of resurrection is infinite. Paul: ‘I consider that our present sufferings are not worth comparing with the glory that will be revealed in us.’ (Romans 8:18)

children.label: Theodicies are ad hoc — no theodicy covers all cases
children.text: Critics argue that theodicies are constructed post-hoc to protect a pre-held belief. Each new category of evil requires a new explanation; together they strain credibility.

children.label: Cumulative theodicy is consistent with how we reason about complex situations
children.text: Multiple explanations for different categories of evil is not ad hoc — it mirrors how we explain complex phenomena in science. The question is whether the explanations are independently plausible, not whether there is only one.
